key_drivers: Falling yield curve trend despite normal regime
reasoning: The macro signals are conflicting. On one hand, the US Treasury yield curve is currently upward sloping (normal), which signifies expectations of economic expansion [57205ed3e4b4]. However, the recent trend of the yield curve is falling (flattening), which is associated with expectations of economic contraction [e0d011fb7082] or a transition from expansion to contraction [aad10797e50f]. This bearish outlook is strongly reinforced by the US 10-year real interest rate, which is high relative to history (86th percentile) and rising; this condition leads to decreased borrowing, lower liquidity, and contractionary GDP [57731d675323, a1a5b29af0a8]. Furthermore, these high real rates are directly linked to the current elevated and rising VIX (86th percentile), as high real rates decrease liquidity and increase equity market volatility [a17543ceb763, 1f895bc8e557]. The confluence of high real rates and rising volatility outweighs the current normal state of the yield curve, leading to a bearish equity outlook.
